condition.qualitative_description: Block-level SOGE extraction exceeds 100% of net annual recharge (Over-exploited) or falls in the Critical class (90–100%), indicating abstraction chronically exceeds recharge in the alluvial aquifer.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: CGWB defines Over-exploited blocks as those where annual groundwater extraction exceeds net annual recharge (SOGE > 100%). In arid alluvial settings such as western Rajasthan and Kachchh, rainfall is below 400 mm and natural recharge is extremely limited, meaning even moderate extraction rates can push blocks into Critical or Over-exploited status. Unlike hard rock aquifers, alluvial aquifers have higher storativity but in arid zones the recharge window is so narrow that depletion accumulates rapidly across years. CGWB data for Rajasthan and Haryana arid belt consistently show large proportions of blocks classified Critical or Over-exploited.

condition.qualitative_description: Well or tubewell depth has increased by more than 5 metres over the available multi-year record, or static water level has declined at a rate exceeding 0.5 m per year, indicating persistent drawdown of the alluvial water table.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Rapid groundwater-level declines exceeding 0.5 m per year are documented as widespread in dry regions with extensive croplands globally, and are particularly acute in arid alluvial plains of northwest India where irrigation demand is high and recharge is minimal. In alluvial aquifers, a sustained decline in static water level forces farmers to deepen tubewells, increasing energy costs and eventually rendering shallow wells non-functional. A 5 m decline over the observation period (~0.6 m/year) in an arid alluvial setting is a strong confirmation of chronic over-extraction.

condition.qualitative_description: Annual delta_g is negative in more than 5 of the last 8 years AND cumulative_g shows a sustained downward trend over the same period, confirming that outflows (ET + extraction + runoff) persistently exceed precipitation-driven recharge.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In arid alluvial zones with rainfall below 400 mm (AER-2) or 400–740 mm (AER-4), the recharge window is narrow and highly episodic. A multi-year negative delta_g combined with a declining groundwater recharge trend confirms that abstraction for irrigation — which accounts for the dominant share of consumptive water use — is drawing down stored alluvial groundwater faster than it is replenished. India has the largest area equipped for groundwater irrigation globally (~39 million ha), and in arid northwest India this extraction pressure is concentrated in a low-recharge environment, making the cumulative depletion signal particularly diagnostic.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 10 cumulative SWC works (check dams, percolation tanks, farm bunds, trench structures) in the micro-watershed over the MGNREGA record, indicating low investment in artificial recharge infrastructure relative to extraction pressure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: India has a long history of tank and pond construction for detaining monsoon runoff to replenish aquifers — over 500,000 such structures exist nationally — and government and NGO investment in percolation tanks in drought-prone arid areas has been substantial. However, where MGNREGA-funded SWC works are sparse, this recharge infrastructure gap amplifies depletion caused by extraction. Low nrega_swc_count signals both an underinvestment in recharge and an intervention opportunity: managed aquifer recharge through streambed structures, check dams and infiltration basins is technically feasible in alluvial plains and has demonstrated recovery potential globally.

overall_reasoning_note: Groundwater stress in arid alluvial aquifers (AER-2, AER-4) should be confirmed by at least two of the three primary signals: elevated SOGE (Critical/Over-exploited), a sustained well-depth deepening trend, or a multi-year negative delta_g with declining groundwater recharge trend. In arid alluvial settings, SOGE is generally more reliable than in hard rock terrain because block boundaries align better with aquifer extent, but it still masks intra-block heterogeneity. The absence of SWC investment (sig_04) amplifies depletion but does not confirm stress on its own. A key distinction from hard rock contexts: alluvial aquifers in arid zones can sustain extraction longer due to higher storativity, but once depleted they recover very slowly without active managed aquifer recharge. Always ask about borewell density and groundwater salinity (see missing_variable_questions) before finalising diagnosis, as saline groundwater is common in Kachchh and parts of Rajasthan and changes the solution pathway significantly.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought causing apparent groundwater stress through reduced recharge rather than structural over-extraction
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Compare annual_delta_g_mm and trend_annual_delta_g_mm against multi-decadal baseline and cross-reference with drought years. If depletion is concentrated in 1–2 severe drought years and SOGE remains below 90% in normal years, drought is the primary driver rather than chronic over-extraction. Check whether well depth recovers partially after good monsoon years — recovery indicates drought-driven stress; continued decline despite good rainfall confirms structural over-extraction.

confounders.confounder: Groundwater salinity rendering available water unusable, mimicking scarcity even where aquifer volume is adequate
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Saline groundwater (common in Kachchh and arid Rajasthan) can cause crop water stress and well abandonment without actual volumetric depletion. If soge_dev_percent is moderate (<70%) but farmers report water insufficiency, ask about salinity (see missing_variable_questions). If salinity is confirmed, the causal pathway shifts from over-extraction depletion to water quality limitation, and solution recommendations change from recharge augmentation to desalination, crop variety substitution, or blending strategies.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Roughly how many borewells or tubewells are operating in your village or the surrounding 2–3 km area? Would you say there are very few (fewer than 10), a moderate number (10–50), or a large number (more than 50)? Have new borewells been drilled in the last 5 years?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: High borewell density in an arid alluvial block with elevated SOGE strongly confirms localised over-extraction. Rapid recent growth in borewell numbers (last 5 years) suggests extraction pressure is accelerating, which would increase diagnostic confidence and urgency of intervention recommendations around collective groundwater governance and MGNREGA-funded recharge works.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers noticed that borewell or well water has become saltier over time, leaves white or crusty deposits on soil or pipes, causes crop leaf burn, or is no longer suitable for certain crops like vegetables or wheat? Has any water testing been done?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: Confirmed salinity in arid alluvial zones (especially Kachchh and western Rajasthan) indicates that the water scarcity stress may be driven by quality limitation rather than volumetric depletion alone. This shifts the primary causal pathway and changes solution recommendations away from recharge augmentation toward water quality management, crop substitution with salt-tolerant varieties, or blending with treated water. It also rules out some recharge solutions that could mobilise saline water into the freshwater lens.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have farmers noticed that well or borewell depth has increased over the past several years? Can you estimate how much deeper wells are now compared to 5–8 years ago (e.g., less than 1 m, 1–3 m, more than 3 m)?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: A reported increase of more than 3 m over 5–8 years strongly confirms groundwater table decline and supports the groundwater stress pathway even when block-level SOGE appears Safe. Stable or shallow well depths suggest extraction pressure or drought rather than structural depletion.

sources.title: Rapid groundwater decline and some cases of recovery in aquifers globally
sources.relevance_note: Documents that rapid groundwater-level declines (>0.5 m/year) are widespread in dry regions with extensive croplands, and that declines have accelerated in 30% of regional aquifers globally; also shows recovery is possible with managed recharge and policy change — directly supports sig_02 threshold and solution recommendations.

sources.title: Groundwater use for irrigation – a global inventory
sources.relevance_note: Establishes India as the country with the largest area equipped for groundwater irrigation globally (~39 million ha), providing context for the scale of extraction pressure in arid alluvial zones; supports sig_03 explanation.

sources.title: Sixty years of global progress in managed aquifer recharge
sources.relevance_note: Documents India's extensive history of tank and percolation pond construction for aquifer recharge (>500,000 structures), government investment in artificial recharge in drought-prone arid areas, and the technical feasibility of MAR in alluvial settings; supports sig_04 explanation and solution recommendations for check dams, trench structures and community ponds.

sources.title: A Snapshot of the World's Groundwater Challenges
sources.relevance_note: Reviews global groundwater depletion dynamics, noting that changing climate amplifies groundwater use and that regions with dense population or intensive agriculture face resource extinction risk; supports the overall framing of chronic depletion in arid agricultural zones and the use of cumulative groundwater stock trends as a diagnostic.

sources.title: Addressing Depletion in Alluvial Aquifers
sources.relevance_note: Argues that alluvial aquifer depletion in India manifests differently from hard rock depletion — as gradual multi-year decline rather than seasonal emptying — and that management approaches must account for geological differences; directly supports the alluvial-specific framing of this card and the need for demand-side governance (Atal Bhujal Yojana, participatory groundwater management) alongside supply-side recharge.

sources.title: National Compilation of Dynamic Ground Water Resources of India 2022
sources.relevance_note: Defines SOGE class thresholds (Safe <70%, Semi-critical 70–90%, Critical 90–100%, Over-exploited >100%) used in sig_01; provides block-level classification data for Rajasthan, Gujarat and Haryana showing high proportions of Critical and Over-exploited blocks in arid alluvial zones.
